title: Person-period hazard model for remaining-laps-to-pit, then collapse to next-lap risk
summary: Discrete-time survival literature fits naturally because `PitNextLap` is exactly a one-step hazard conditioned on not having pitted yet in the current stint.
rationale: Discrete-time survival literature fits naturally because `PitNextLap` is exactly a one-step hazard conditioned on not having pitted yet in the current stint. Public pit-stop work also reports value from joint classification plus stop-horizon estimation, suggesting the label should be modeled as part of a time-to-event process rather than only as a flat binary target.
implementation_hint: Rebuild each stint as person-period data from current lap until pit/censor, fit a discrete hazard model with lap-bin/stint-age terms and time-varying covariates, then score the current interval’s hazard as `P(pit next lap)`. Test a tree-based hazard model first, and optionally add a second head for remaining laps to stop as an auxiliary target.
expected_effect: Better handling of pit-window shape and censoring, especially near the end of stints where plain binary models can blur ‘not yet’ vs ‘not this stint’.
risk: Data expansion can be expensive, and poorly defined censoring at race end or weather-regime changes can hurt more than help.
